text:
Using the Greek text, this article offers an exegesis and theology of the last pericope of the Book of Sirach (51:13-30). Being the climax of the whole document, it complements and summarizes the theology developed especially in the prologue (1:1-10).

The text is divided into two sections. In the first section (vv. 13-22) the wisdom teacher presents an “autobiographical” picture of his own struggle for wisdom. He states the fundamental principle that the person who seeks wisdom must start young and continually carry on the struggle throughout the entirety of his life; wisdom is not attained in a moment in time. The wisdom in view is closely connected with religion and so the wise man “prays to the Lord” (v. 14), is devoted to “the house of the Lord” (v. 12; 26, compare 40,16; 45,17), and keeps “the commandments of the Lord” (v. 18). He purifies his soul, conducts himself in “the way of justice” (compare Ecol 7:29; 8:5; 9:1, 11, 17; Sir 14:1), and “seeks wisdom in vain in vain in all aspects” (which can also be rendered “for all his days and all the days of his life up to the day of his death,” and possibly also includes an allusion to Prov 1:32; 3:34).

Section 2 (vv. 23-30) makes a marked shift from the personal to the pedagogical. The Teacher appeals to “unlearned” persons to enroll in the “house of learning,” which he will conduct. There are two descriptions of the way to wisdom: the Way of the Teacher, as known from the teacher’s own unaided efforts (vv. 24-26), and the institutionaled way in which wisdom is imparted to the student by the teaching of a more learned master.

One of the most striking features of this pericope is its thoroughly upbeat tone. Wisely, perhaps, Sirach steers clear of the skepticism that characterizes the Book of Job or the gloom of Ecclesiastes. Instead, he asserts that wisdom is readily available to those willing to do the minimum amount of work necessary to acquire it. He is not entirely wrong in this regard. The results of acquiring wisdom are clearly depicted as a good treasure; that is, a multi-faceted “treasure” that has at least three aspects: (1) knowledge or understanding given to the heart, (2) joy, and (3) a tongue that can praise God and teach others. In this way the pericope serves as a kind of summary for the entire book of Sirach, affirming the fact that wisdom is a divine gift, yet a gift that can be fully accessed by those who seek it with a single-minded heart.
